text: The Stoics used the metaphor of the cylinder to explain why assent remains free even when impressions are compelling. A cylinder set rolling needs an initial push, but thereafter rolls by its own nature — its shape determines its motion. Chrysippus argued that impressions provide the push; the character of the soul provides the shape. A person of weak character, upon receiving an enticing impression, rolls far in the direction of desire. A person of trained reason rolls less — or comes to rest. The push cannot be prevented, but neither is it decisive. Moral training is therefore not the avoidance of temptation but the reshaping of the cylinder itself.
question: In the cylinder metaphor, what does the shape of the cylinder represent?

options.text: The strength of the original impression that sets the cylinder rolling
The circumstances of the external environment
The memory of how past similar impulses resolved
The character and trained nature of the soul
